definition: Each recipient receives an identical share regardless of need, contribution, or status.
Goods are distributed in proportion to recipients' needs; those who need more receive more.
Goods are distributed in proportion to contribution, effort, or desert; more input earns more.
Inequalities are just only insofar as they maximally benefit the least-advantaged (maximin).
Justice requires that everyone reach a threshold of enough; distribution above it matters less.
Positions are open to all and people with equal talent and motivation have equal life-chances, correcting social contingencies.
Inequalities from brute luck are unjust and to be neutralized; inequalities from genuine choice are acceptable.
Justice is the distribution of real freedoms (capabilities) to achieve valued functionings, not resources or welfare.
Fairness of the decision-making process itself: consistency, voice, accuracy, correctability, absence of bias.
Fairness of the dignity and respect with which people are treated when procedures are enacted.
Fairness of the explanations and information provided about procedures and outcomes.
Wrongdoing deserves punishment proportional to the offence; the backward-looking justice of deserved sanction.
Response to harm centred on repairing relationships and reintegration through encounter, not proportional punishment.
Restoration of a balance disturbed by a wrongful transaction between parties (Aristotle's diorthotic justice).
Victims are made whole for losses wrongfully imposed on them; the justice of compensation.
The fair distribution of advantages and burdens across society's institutions and structures.
Fairness in voluntary exchanges and transactions between parties (as distinct from distribution).
Rawls' overall conception: principles of justice are those that would be chosen behind a veil of ignorance.
Different social goods belong to distinct spheres, each with its own just distributive criterion; domination is injustice.
Justice as the recognition of identity and status, addressing misrecognition rather than (only) maldistribution.
Justice consists in people relating to one another as equals, not in any distributive pattern as such.
Holdings are just if acquired and transferred by just steps; justice is historical, not patterned or end-state.
A distribution is just if it maximizes aggregate welfare; justice is the greatest good summed over persons.
Like cases must be treated alike and unlike cases differently; the formal principle of impartial consistency.
